blueprint_refs: Policy: "advance by sampled dwell_time plus travel_time to next POI (non-decreasing; must not exceed day boundary)"
Policy: "Generate next timestamp via travel_time(distance)+dwell_time; enforce feasibility (non-decreasing; within day boundary)."

evidence.metrics: Many generated day logs show repeated identical timestamps within a day (e.g., multiple events at 23:33:00; other logs with repeated 22:04:00/23:20:00 blocks).

trajectory-timestamps-not-strictly-incre.error_identification: Simulated trajectories frequently contain multiple POI visits at the exact same HH:MM:SS within a day, indicating time-bunching rather than a plausible ordered schedule.
trajectory-timestamps-not-strictly-incre.root_cause_analysis: In MobilitySimulator.simulate_one_day, the computed event start_time is clamped into the active window and can equal previous events because dwell can push current_time near aw_hi and later events are forced to start at aw_hi (or at max(arrival,target_start) which is then clamped). There is no final enforcement of strictly increasing minutes across out_pairs, only sorting during parsing of ground truth. This violates the intended sequential timing mechanism in the blueprint and produces degenerate time histograms.
trajectory-timestamps-not-strictly-incre.correct_approach: Enforce monotonicity at emission time: before appending an event, set start_time = max(start_time, last_start_time + 1) (minute resolution), and if that exceeds aw_hi or 1439, stop planning further events (or reduce planned stops earlier). Additionally, when arrival > aw_hi, break instead of clamping to aw_hi; and incorporate remaining-time feasibility using sampled dwell/travel (not just typical estimates) to prevent late-day bunching.
trajectory-timestamps-not-strictly-incre.key_insight: If output format allows non-decreasing times but realism/metrics need separation, always enforce a minimum increment (e.g., +1 minute) between successive events and terminate the day when the schedule cannot remain feasible—never ‘clamp’ multiple events to the same boundary minute.

blueprint_refs: Parameters: "dwell_time_params_by_category" and "travel_time_scale" tuned to align with observed time gaps

evidence.metrics: stop_count_abs_mean_error=1.352 and stop_count_kl=0.547 indicate stop-count distribution mismatch.

stop-count-model-misspecified-uses-only-.error_identification: Stops per day are systematically off: average absolute mean error ~1.35 stops/day and KL ~0.55 vs validation distribution.
stop-count-model-misspecified-uses-only-.root_cause_analysis: learn_agent_priors estimates base_dwell_lognorm from gap_minutes_by_cat, but gap is computed as (next_event.minute - current_event.minute) using start times only. This mixes dwell+travel and also includes frequent zero/near-zero gaps caused by identical timestamps in data or sorting, biasing dwell low and making the simulator able to pack more events into the active window. The simulator then further shrinks planned stops using a coarse typical feasibility check, disconnecting stop-count from calibrated dwell/travel dynamics.
stop-count-model-misspecified-uses-only-.correct_approach: Treat inter-event gaps as a separate distribution from dwell: (1) estimate an empirical inter-event gap model per category (or per category transition) from TRAIN, optionally with a floor >0 and robust trimming of 0-minute gaps; (2) in simulation, update time by sampling a gap directly (or sampling dwell + travel with dwell calibrated independently). Then the stop-count can be derived from an end-of-day hazard or from the gap process, rather than a separately sampled planned count that gets heuristically truncated.
stop-count-model-misspecified-uses-only-.key_insight: Don’t infer dwell from start-to-start gaps unless you explicitly model travel and handle zero-gap artifacts; otherwise you’ll miscalibrate both stop counts and time-of-day placement.

blueprint_refs: Policy: "next-activity coarse category choice conditioned on time-of-day and day-type"

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.334; worst categories logged: College & University=0.639, Event=0.594, Residence=0.534.

category-time-of-day-fit-poor-in-specifi.error_identification: Time-of-day distributions by category remain poorly matched (avg JSD ~0.334) with particularly bad categories (College & University, Event, Residence).
category-time-of-day-fit-poor-in-specifi.root_cause_analysis: Category timing is driven by per-category histograms plus a single global tod_logit_weight. However, (a) categories with sparse data rely on smoothed near-uniform histograms, (b) the target-minute resampling enforces m>=current_minute and within a broad active window, which can shift mass late, and (c) Residence is frequently inserted via p_start_home and p_end_home logic plus late-day clamping, concentrating Residence times. These mechanisms together distort per-category timing relative to validation.
category-time-of-day-fit-poor-in-specifi.correct_approach: Add category-specific timing strength or backoff: for sparse categories, blend priors.cat_time_hist with a global (or agent-level) generic hist using an adaptive mixing weight based on effective sample size. For Residence, separate 'home anchor' logic from category timing hist: ensure home events are placed at empirically learned start/end distributions (weekday/weekend) and avoid clamping-induced late spikes. Also consider lowering tod_logit_weight for categories with high JSD via per-category weights (still calibratable but constrained).
category-time-of-day-fit-poor-in-specifi.key_insight: A single global time-of-day weight is rarely sufficient; use adaptive backoff based on category data volume and treat ‘home’ as a special anchor process instead of a normal category histogram.

blueprint_refs: Agent attribute: "poi_preference_weights" from TRAIN days only; used in POI choice utility
Policy: "Choose specific POI using utility combining personal POI affinity ..."

evidence.metrics: topk_poi_recall=0.325 (mean overlap between GT top-10 POIs and simulated POI set per agent is low).

topk-poi-recall-low-indicates-insufficie.error_identification: Simulated trajectories often miss agents’ frequent POIs (top-k recall ~0.325).
topk-poi-recall-low-indicates-insufficie.root_cause_analysis: _choose_poi builds a large candidate set including near/explore pools and gives a flat seen_poi_bonus to all seen POIs. This can drown out the relative ordering among an agent’s true top POIs, especially when the candidate set includes many exploration POIs with adequate distance and transition scores. Additionally, poi_prob is smoothed over a support that includes a potentially large exploration tail, diluting mass on top POIs.
topk-poi-recall-low-indicates-insufficie.correct_approach: Make exploration controlled and preference-preserving: (1) reduce exploration_tail size or sample it conditionally by category and time-of-day; (2) replace flat seen_poi_bonus with a rank-based or log-probability-based bonus (e.g., bonus proportional to log poi_prob or only for top-N seen POIs); (3) cap candidate_set more aggressively around top-pref POIs plus a small exploration budget; (4) during calibration, include topk_poi_recall as an auxiliary objective (already computed) if allowed by your evaluation harness.
topk-poi-recall-low-indicates-insufficie.key_insight: Personalization metrics degrade when exploration is added as a broad tail; keep exploration as a small, explicit budget and never flatten preference rankings with uniform bonuses.

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=0.417 but gt_step_distance_missing_ratio=0.696 vs sim_step_distance_missing_ratio=0.010, implying comparisons use very different step subsets.

distance-metric-interpretability-broken-.error_identification: Step-distance metric looks reasonable (0.417 Wasserstein), but ground truth distance coverage is extremely low (~30% of steps have coords) while simulated coverage is high (~99%), making the distance distribution comparison potentially biased and overconfident.
distance-metric-interpretability-broken-.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator correctly drops missing-coordinate steps for both GT and SIM, but because SIM tends to pick POIs with known coordinates (from poi_id_to_latlon universe) and GT contains many POIs missing from the POI coordinate file, the compared distributions are not matched on the same support. This can also indirectly affect category composition if missingness correlates with certain POI types.
distance-metric-interpretability-broken-.correct_approach: Make the distance metric comparable by restricting SIM step-distance evaluation to the subset of POI IDs that have coordinates in GT validation (or by computing a matched-coverage metric: evaluate distances only for (agent,day) steps where both consecutive GT POIs have coords, and compute SIM distances over the corresponding simulated consecutive steps after mapping to the same positions in the sequence). If you cannot align step indices, at least report an additional diagnostic: fraction of simulated POIs that appear in GT coordinate-covered set for that agent/day.
distance-metric-interpretability-broken-.key_insight: When a metric drops missing values asymmetrically between GT and SIM, you must align supports (or condition evaluation on comparable subsets), otherwise the metric can be ‘good’ while the behavior is not.

main-executes-on-import-causes-integrati.error_identification: The script calls main() unconditionally at module import, which can break reuse in larger systems or unit tests and makes it harder to run partial components (e.g., parsing-only validation) safely.
main-executes-on-import-causes-integrati.root_cause_analysis: At the end of CURRENT CODE, main() is invoked directly rather than behind a standard if __name__ == '__main__' guard, causing side effects on import and limiting debuggability.
main-executes-on-import-causes-integrati.correct_approach: Wrap the main() call in an if __name__ == '__main__': block. This preserves CLI behavior while allowing the simulator, parser, evaluator, and calibrator to be imported and tested independently.
main-executes-on-import-causes-integrati.key_insight: Simulation pipelines should be import-safe; always guard top-level execution so components can be reused, tested, and instrumented without triggering a full run.
